title: Structural Welding, Advanced Processes & Fabrication Design

required.title: [Reference] AWS D1.1 Structural Welding Code — Steel
[Equipment] MIG welder (as in Weathering Storms), plus stick welder (AC/DC capable, 225 amp minimum) and ideally a TIG welder (AC/DC for aluminum and steel)
[Equipment] Welding positioner or fixture table for out-of-position welding practice
[Tools] Layout tools (combination square, protractor, scriber, center punch), measuring tools (calipers, tape measure), fabrication clamps, welding magnets, levels
[Materials] Various steel stock (flat bar, angle, tubing, plate in multiple thicknesses), stainless steel and aluminum for TIG practice, stick electrodes (6010, 6013, 7018)

suggested.title: [Book]
suggested.author: T.B. Jefferson and Gorham Woods (Lincoln Electric)

suggested.title: [Video] YouTube: Weld.com — out-of-position welding and TIG welding tutorials

supplementary.title: [Reference] ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (Section IX)

competence_markers.description: The student can produce acceptable welds in flat, horizontal, vertical-up, and overhead positions with MIG
The student can produce acceptable structural welds with stick welding (7018 electrode) in at least flat and horizontal positions
The student has been introduced to TIG welding and can produce acceptable beads on flat plate
The student can read fabrication drawings including weld symbols and work to specified tolerances
The student has designed and built a jig and used it to produce multiple identical parts
The student can identify failure modes (fatigue, corrosion, brittle fracture, overload) in failed metal components
The student has designed, drawn, fabricated, and load-tested a substantial structural project
The student can teach fabrication skills to a less experienced student effectively and safely

content.why_this_matters:
At Weathering Storms, you learned to weld in flat position and build simple fabricated projects. Now you step into the domain where fabrication becomes engineering. You will weld in all positions — horizontal, vertical, and overhead — because real-world structures do not arrange themselves conveniently on a flat table. You will learn additional welding processes — stick (SMAW) and TIG (GTAW) — because different situations demand different tools. You will work to specified tolerances on drawings, design jigs and fixtures that ensure repeatability, and analyze metal failures to understand why things break so you can build things that do not.

Structural welding means welding that people depend on with their safety. A welded frame that carries a load, a trailer hitch that tows a vehicle, a handrail that someone leans on at the top of stairs — every structural weld is a promise that the joint will hold. A bad structural weld is a lie that will eventually be exposed, and the consequences can be catastrophic. The standard for structural welding is not "it looks good." The standard is "it will hold under the worst load it could reasonably encounter, with a margin of safety."

You will also learn to teach fabrication — to supervise a less experienced student through a project, ensuring safety, quality, and understanding. Teaching is the capstone test of your knowledge: if you can explain the physics of welding, demonstrate proper technique, diagnose someone else's defects, and guide them to improvement, you have mastered this trade at a level that is genuinely useful to others.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **All-position welding** — producing consistent, quality welds in flat, horizontal, vertical, and overhead positions
2. **Stick welding (SMAW)** — the most versatile welding process, essential for field work and adverse conditions
3. **TIG welding (GTAW)** — the precision process for thin materials, stainless steel, aluminum, and high-quality applications
4. **Tolerance-based fabrication** — working to specified dimensions on drawings where parts must fit together precisely
5. **Jig and fixture design** — creating devices that hold workpieces in correct position for repeatable, accurate production
6. **Failure analysis** — identifying fatigue, corrosion, brittle fracture, and overload in failed metal components
7. **Fabrication design** — designing structures and assemblies from concept through detailed drawings to finished product
8. **Teaching fabrication** — supervising less experienced students through projects with safety, quality, and understanding
content.core_content:
### Part 1: All-Position Welding

In the real world, weld joints exist in every orientation. A structural frame has horizontal joints, vertical joints, and — if you are welding overhead beams — overhead joints. Each position changes how gravity interacts with the molten weld puddle, and you must adjust your technique accordingly.

**Flat position (1F/1G):** You already know this. Gravity pulls the puddle into the joint. The easiest position. Your baseline for quality.

**Horizontal position (2F/2G):** The joint axis is horizontal, but the weld face is vertical. Gravity pulls the puddle downward, away from the upper plate. You must angle the gun slightly upward to counteract gravity and keep the puddle centered on the joint. Travel speed may need to increase slightly to prevent the puddle from sagging.

**Vertical position (3F/3G):** The joint axis is vertical. Gravity pulls the puddle straight down. Two approaches:
- *Vertical up:* You weld from bottom to top, fighting gravity. The puddle wants to run downward. You use a weaving or stepping motion — move the gun slightly side to side or pause briefly at each side of the joint to allow the puddle to solidify enough to support the next deposit. Vertical up produces better penetration and is the standard for structural work.
- *Vertical down:* You weld from top to bottom, working with gravity. Faster but with less penetration. Acceptable for non-structural sheet metal work but generally not for structural joints.

**Overhead position (4F/4G):** The most challenging position. You are welding on the underside of the joint. Gravity pulls the puddle directly toward you. You must work with a smaller puddle (lower heat settings), maintain a tight arc length, and use a stringer bead technique (straight line, no weaving) to keep the puddle from dripping. Overhead welding produces more spatter — ensure your PPE coverage is complete, including covering your collar to prevent hot spatter from falling down your shirt.

**The practice requirement:** You must produce acceptable welds in all four positions before undertaking structural fabrication. This is not arbitrary — any real structure will present joints in multiple orientations, and the quality must be consistent throughout. A frame with excellent flat welds and defective vertical welds is a structure with a weak point that will be found by the first serious load.

### Part 2: Stick Welding (SMAW)

Stick welding — Shielded Metal Arc Welding — is the oldest and most versatile arc welding process. It uses a consumable electrode: a metal rod coated in flux. The arc melts both the rod and the base metal. The flux coating melts and produces a gas shield and a slag layer that protect the weld from atmospheric contamination.

**Why learn stick welding when you already know MIG?** Because MIG requires clean metal, shielding gas, a wire feed mechanism, and electrical power. Stick welding works on rusty metal, dirty metal, wet metal (not ideal, but functional). It works in wind that would blow away MIG shielding gas. It works in remote locations where you can run a generator and carry a box of rods. It works on cast iron, on dissimilar metals, on heavy plate that MIG struggles to penetrate. Stick welding is the field welder's process — the one that works when conditions are not ideal.

**Electrode selection:**
- *6010:* Deep penetration, fast-freeze slag. Excellent for root passes (the first weld in a groove joint) and for dirty or rusty metal. Requires DC+ and produces a rough bead with more spatter. The "get it done" electrode.
- *6013:* Medium penetration, easy arc start, smooth bead. Good for general-purpose work on clean metal. Works on AC or DC. A good learning electrode.
- *7018:* Low hydrogen, smooth arc, excellent mechanical properties. The standard structural electrode — specified for most structural welding applications. Requires DC+ and must be stored dry (moisture in the flux coating introduces hydrogen, which causes cracking). The electrode you will use most for quality structural work.

**Technique differences from MIG:** In MIG welding, you push the gun in the direction of travel. In stick welding, you drag the electrode — the rod points back toward the completed weld. The arc length (distance from the rod tip to the puddle) must be maintained manually — approximately equal to the electrode diameter. Too long an arc loses shielding and produces porosity. Too short an arc causes the rod to stick to the workpiece.

The slag produced by stick welding must be chipped and brushed off between passes. Never weld over slag — it creates inclusions (slag trapped inside the weld) that weaken the joint. Chip thoroughly, wire brush, inspect, then lay the next pass.

### Part 3: TIG Welding (GTAW) Introduction

TIG welding — Gas Tungsten Arc Welding — is the precision process. A non-consumable tungsten electrode creates the arc. Shielding gas (pure argon) protects the weld. Filler metal is added manually with a separate rod held in the other hand. The welder controls the arc with a foot pedal that modulates current in real time.

TIG welding demands both hands and a foot working in independent coordination — one hand holds the torch, one hand feeds filler rod, and the foot controls amperage. It is the most difficult welding process to learn and the most precise once mastered.

**Where TIG excels:**
- Thin materials (sheet metal, tubing) where MIG and stick would burn through
- Stainless steel and aluminum, where weld appearance and quality must be exceptional
- Any application where the weld will be visible and must be cosmetically perfect
- Pipe welding, especially root passes in open-root groove joints
- Dissimilar metals and exotic alloys

**Learning progression:** TIG is introduced at Standing Ground but not expected to reach the same proficiency as MIG or stick within this packet. The student should be able to run acceptable beads on flat plate (steel and, if equipment allows, aluminum), weld simple butt and fillet joints, and understand when TIG is the appropriate process selection. Mastery of TIG is a journey of years, not weeks.

### Part 4: Fabrication to Tolerances

Professional fabrication works to drawings. Drawings specify dimensions, tolerances, material specifications, weld symbols, and finish requirements. The fabricator's job is to produce a physical object that matches the drawing within the specified tolerances.

**Tolerance** is the allowable deviation from the specified dimension. A dimension of 12.000" +/- 0.030" means the part must be between 11.970" and 12.030" long. A tighter tolerance (+/- 0.010") costs more because it requires more careful work. A looser tolerance (+/- 0.125") allows faster work but produces a less precise result.

**Cumulative tolerance:** This is where most fabrication errors originate. If you have ten cuts, each with a tolerance of +/- 1/16", the total accumulated error can be up to 10/16" (5/8") in the worst case. This means a 10-foot-long assembly made from ten 12-inch pieces could be off by over half an inch. Professional fabrication manages cumulative tolerance by: working from a single reference point (measuring all dimensions from one end, not from the previous cut), using master dimensions (the overall length, not the individual pieces, is the controlling dimension), and checking frequently against the drawing as assembly progresses.

**Weld symbols:** Fabrication drawings use standardized weld symbols (AWS A2.4) to specify the type, size, and location of each weld. The basic weld symbol tells you: the type of joint (fillet, groove, plug, etc.), the size of the weld (leg size for fillets, depth for grooves), which side of the joint the weld goes on (arrow side or other side), and any special requirements (field weld, weld all around, etc.). Learning to read weld symbols is learning to read the fabricator's language.

### Part 5: Jig and Fixture Design

A jig is a device that holds a workpiece in the correct position during welding, drilling, or assembly. A fixture holds work during machining. Together, they ensure that every piece produced is identical — the same dimensions, the same angles, the same weld locations.

**Why jigs matter:** Without a jig, every assembly must be measured, squared, and clamped individually. This takes time and introduces variability. With a jig, you place the parts in the jig, clamp, and weld. The jig ensures the result is correct. If you are making one bracket, measuring and clamping individually is fine. If you are making twenty brackets, a jig saves hours and produces more consistent results.

**Jig design principles:**
- The jig must locate the workpiece accurately — pins, stops, or shaped surfaces that force the piece into the correct position
- The jig must clamp the workpiece securely — toggle clamps, C-clamps, or built-in clamping mechanisms that hold the piece during welding or machining
- The jig must allow access to the weld joints — you cannot weld a joint you cannot reach with the gun
- The jig must be more rigid than the workpiece — it should not flex under clamping or welding forces
- The jig must account for weld distortion — parts that will shrink toward the weld as they cool may need to be pre-set slightly past their final position

Designing a jig requires you to think about the entire production process, not just the individual part. This is where fabrication becomes engineering.

### Part 6: Failure Analysis

Understanding how metal fails is understanding how to prevent failure. You will examine failed metal components and identify the failure mode.

**Fatigue failure:** Cyclic loading — repeated application and removal of force — eventually cracks metal. A paperclip bent back and forth breaks by fatigue. A metal bracket that vibrates for years can crack by fatigue. Fatigue cracks typically start at a stress concentration — a sharp corner, a notch, a weld toe, a surface scratch — and propagate slowly through the material until the remaining cross-section cannot carry the load, at which point the part fails suddenly. Fatigue fracture surfaces show characteristic "beach marks" — concentric rings spreading outward from the crack origin, visible to the naked eye on many fractures.

**Corrosion:** Environmental degradation of metal. Rust (iron oxide) forms when steel is exposed to oxygen and moisture. Galvanic corrosion occurs when dissimilar metals are in contact in the presence of an electrolyte (water) — one metal corrodes preferentially. Stress corrosion cracking occurs under the combined action of tensile stress and a corrosive environment. Corrosion reduces the cross-section of the metal, weakening it until it fails.

**Brittle fracture:** Sudden failure without significant deformation. Some metals become brittle at low temperatures — steel that is ductile at room temperature can fail catastrophically at -40 degrees. The heat-affected zone (HAZ) near a weld can become brittle if the welding procedure allows the metal to cool too quickly. Brittle fracture surfaces are flat and shiny (crystalline appearance), unlike ductile fracture surfaces which are rough and fibrous.

**Overload:** The applied force simply exceeded the material's capacity. Overload failure shows significant deformation (bending, stretching, necking) before the final break. The fracture surface is rough and fibrous. Overload is the simplest failure mode — the part was not strong enough for the load. The design was inadequate, or the load exceeded what was anticipated.

**Weld-specific failures:** Lack of fusion (the weld did not actually fuse to the base metal), lack of penetration (the weld did not reach the root of the joint), porosity (gas trapped in the weld created voids), and hydrogen cracking (hydrogen from moisture in the flux or atmosphere caused delayed cracking in the heat-affected zone). Each of these failures has specific causes and specific prevention measures.

### Part 7: Teaching Fabrication

Teaching a less experienced student to fabricate requires you to:
- Plan the project at an appropriate level for the student's skills
- Ensure all safety protocols are followed (PPE, fire prevention, ventilation)
- Demonstrate each technique before the student attempts it
- Observe the student's work in real time and correct errors before they compound
- Evaluate the finished work against specific standards and provide honest, constructive feedback
- Explain the physics behind the technique so the student understands why, not just what

The teaching experience will expose gaps in your own understanding. When the student asks "why does the bead look different when I change the angle?" you must be able to explain arc physics, puddle behavior, and heat input in terms the student can understand. If you cannot explain it, you do not fully understand it yourself — and that discovery is valuable.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. All-Position Welding
**Developing well:** The student produces welds in all four positions that meet visual inspection criteria consistently. They adjust technique appropriately for each position — travel speed, gun angle, weave pattern. Their vertical-up and overhead welds, while potentially showing less refinement than their flat welds, still meet structural quality standards.

**Developing slowly:** Out-of-position welds show severe defects — excessive drip in overhead, lack of fusion in vertical, inconsistent profile in horizontal. The student has not adjusted technique for position and is using flat-position habits in positions where they do not work.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student had enough practice time in each position? (Out-of-position welding requires significantly more practice than flat.) Are the machine settings adjusted for position? (Lower heat for overhead, slightly higher for vertical-up.) Is the student's body position stable? (Out-of-position welding requires creative positioning of the body to maintain a stable, comfortable welding posture.)

### 2. Stick Welding
**Developing well:** The student maintains a stable arc with 7018 electrodes, produces consistent bead profiles, and properly cleans slag between passes. They understand electrode selection for different applications and can articulate why 7018 is the structural standard.

**Developing slowly:** The student cannot maintain arc length as the electrode shortens. The rod sticks frequently. Slag removal is incomplete, leading to inclusions in subsequent passes.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student's amperage setting correct? (Too low makes maintaining the arc difficult; too high burns through.) Have they practiced enough arc starts and restarts? (The restart, where you must strike the arc in front of the crater and move back into it, is a specific skill that needs drilling.)

### 3. Fabrication Precision
**Developing well:** The student's fabricated projects match their drawings within specified tolerances. They manage cumulative tolerance by measuring from reference points. They check dimensions during assembly, not just at the end.

**Developing slowly:** Projects do not match drawings. The student cuts all pieces individually and assembles, discovering at the end that the accumulated error has produced a result significantly off dimension.

**Diagnostic questions:** Does the student understand cumulative tolerance conceptually? Have you demonstrated the difference between measuring from a reference point versus measuring each piece from the previous piece?

### 4. Failure Analysis
**Developing well:** The student correctly identifies failure modes from physical evidence. They can distinguish fatigue beach marks from brittle fracture crystalline surfaces from ductile overload fibrous surfaces. They can reason backward from the failure to the probable cause.

**Developing slowly:** The student looks at a broken piece and cannot distinguish one failure mode from another. They guess rather than reason from evidence.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you provided clear examples of each failure mode? Has the student examined enough failed components to develop pattern recognition? Consider purchasing or making samples of each failure mode for repeated study.

### 5. Teaching Effectiveness
**Developing well:** The student plans an appropriate lesson, demonstrates clearly, maintains safety, catches and corrects errors constructively, and produces a learner who can complete the task. They explain the physics and reasoning behind the technique.

**Developing slowly:** The student teaches at their own level, demonstrates too fast, uses vocabulary the learner does not know, or becomes frustrated when the learner makes mistakes.

**Diagnostic questions:** Did the student plan the lesson in advance? Did they anticipate the learner's likely difficulties? Can they explain why they do what they do, or do they only know the motions without the reasoning?
